title: Naples-Style Meatballs in Red Sauce (Allegro-Inspired, Research-Modified)

Sauce: 2 (28 oz) cans whole peeled San Marzano tomatoes (DOP preferred), hand-crushed with 1 cup reserved uncooked for late addition
1/4 cup high-quality extra virgin olive oil, plus more for finishing
4 cloves garlic, hand-chopped (not pressed or microplaned)
1 small fresh chili pepper (Calabrian or Fresno), left whole for removable infusion
3-4 fresh basil stems for simmering, plus a handful of fresh basil leaves for finishing
2 teaspoons salt, adjusted to taste in the final hour
Fresh Italian parsley, chopped, for finishing
Meatballs: 4 thick slices day-old Italian or white bread, crusts removed, torn into pieces
1/2 cup whole milk, for soaking the bread
1 cup freshly grated Pecorino Romano cheese (not pre-grated)

steps: Build the panade: Tear the crustless bread into small pieces and place in a bowl. Pour the milk over the bread, stir gently, and let soak for at least 15 minutes. The bread should break down into a soft, spreadable paste. Squeeze out excess milk before using.

Crush the tomatoes: Pour both cans of San Marzano tomatoes into a large bowl. Crush by hand into irregular chunks — some smooth, some coarse. Reserve about 1 cup of the crushed tomatoes in a separate bowl and set aside. This reserved portion will be added late in the cook to restore brightness lost during the long simmer.

Sweat the soffritto: Heat the olive oil in a large heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven over medium-low heat. Add the diced onion and cook, stirring occasionally, until fully soft and translucent — about 8 minutes. Do not let it brown. Add the hand-chopped garlic and cook about 1 minute more until fragrant.

Fry the tomato paste: Push the aromatics to the edges of the pot. Add the tomato paste to the center and stir it into the oil. Cook, stirring frequently, for about 3 minutes until it darkens to a brick color and smells sweet and concentrated.
Deglaze with wine: Pour in the white wine and stir well, scraping up any fond from the pot bottom. Let it reduce by roughly half, about 2 minutes.

Add tomatoes and begin the long simmer: Add the main portion of hand-crushed tomatoes (not the reserved cup) and all their juices to the pot. Stir in the salt and black pepper. Nestle the whole fresh chili pepper and the basil stems into the sauce. Bring to a gentle boil, then immediately drop to the lowest possible simmer. Cover with the lid slightly ajar. Simmer for 3 to 4 hours, stirring gently every 30-45 minutes to prevent sticking. If the sauce over-thickens, add a small splash of wine or water.

Mix the meatballs (start about 1 hour before the sauce is done): Combine the ground beef and ground pork in a large bowl. Add the eggs, the soaked bread panade (squeezed of excess milk), the Pecorino Romano, minced garlic, parsley, salt, pepper, and red pepper flakes. Mix gently with your hands until just combined — stop the moment everything is incorporated. Overworking produces tough, dense meatballs.
Shape the meatballs: With damp hands, gently roll the mixture into golf ball-sized meatballs (roughly 2 inches). You should get about 16-20 meatballs. Handle them as little as possible.

Add meatballs to the sauce for the final simmer: When the sauce has simmered for 3 to 4 hours and has developed deep flavor, remove and discard the basil stems and the whole chili pepper. Gently lower the raw meatballs into the simmering sauce one at a time. Push them down so they are mostly submerged. Cover with the lid slightly ajar and simmer gently for 45 minutes to 1 hour, until the meatballs are cooked through. Do not stir aggressively — gently shake the pot or use a spoon to nudge, not flip.

Restore brightness and finish: About 15 minutes before serving, stir in the reserved cup of uncooked crushed San Marzano tomatoes. This restores the volatile fresh tomato aromatics that the long simmer cooked out. Taste and adjust salt. Tear fresh basil leaves over the top, scatter chopped parsley, and drizzle with a thread of your best extra virgin olive oil. Serve over pasta or with crusty bread, passing extra freshly grated Pecorino Romano at the table.
